one_line_takeaway: An early, secondary-source synthesis that organizes prompt-injection attacks into a two-branch / ten-class taxonomy (6 direct + 4 indirect) meant as a vulnerability checklist, contributing the subcategory classes on top of the cited direct/indirect split rather than any new attack or defense evaluation.
key_finding: The authors identified and (for direct types) verified seventeen distinct prompt-injection variations and argue they cluster cleanly into a two-branch, ten-class taxonomy (6 direct + 4 indirect classes). They conclude that safeguards at the interface layer are easily bypassed with relatively simple direct injections, that building a fully safe LLM interface may be practically impossible (even for premier AI labs), and that indirect injections are the more serious emerging threat and the primary risk to ordinary end users. They frame the taxonomy as a checklist for systematically probing LLM interfaces and call for a standardized battery of injection tests analogous to how SQL-injection-safe database practices developed.
core_contribution: An exploratory taxonomy paper that compiles and organizes known prompt injection attacks on LLMs into a two-level categorization. The paper's OWN contribution is the set of fine-grained subcategory 'classes': six classes of direct prompt injection (Double Character, Virtualization, Obfuscation, Payload Splitting, Adversarial Suffix, Instruction Manipulation) and four classes of indirect prompt injection (Active, Passive, User-driven, Virtual Prompt Injection), together with a per-class table of name/target/objective and a compiled example list intended as a developer/end-user vulnerability checklist. Importantly, the higher-level direct-vs-indirect split is NOT this paper's contribution; it is explicitly adopted from cited prior work (Greshake et al., 2023). Individual attack techniques (goal hijacking and prompt leaking from Perez & Ribeiro 2022; payload splitting and obfuscation from Kang et al. 2023; adversarial suffixes from Zou et al. 2023; virtual prompt injection / data poisoning from Yan et al. 2023) are likewise drawn from cited work; the paper's value-add is the synthesis, naming, and organizing of these into a single mutually-distinguished class structure.
threat_model: Adversary attempts to bypass the safety/alignment safeguards layered in an LLM interface (API or chatbot), not the raw model. Two attacker postures: (1) DIRECT - the attacker interacts with the LLM directly and crafts prompts to circumvent output restrictions (produce hate speech, malware, illegal-activity instructions, adult content) or to reveal/override the system's initial/instruction prompt. (2) INDIRECT - the attacker plants instructions in content that an LLM-integrated application later ingests (email an LLM assistant reads, a webpage a browser plugin summarizes, training/instruction-tuning data), so the LLM executes attacker instructions the victim never sees; goals resemble traditional cyberattacks (data exfiltration, forwarding emails to the attacker, misinformation, steering model behavior via poisoning). The interface/safeguard layer is the defended asset; the LLM's inputs, ingested content, and training data are the attack vectors.
limitations: Explicitly non-exhaustive and non-experimental: no attack-success-rate measurements, no defense evaluation, and no reproducible artifact of the corpus. The categorization is a snapshot of a fast-moving field and the authors expect classes to be added or become obsolete. Heavy reliance on non-peer-reviewed grey-literature sources (blogs, Reddit, Twitter, jailbreakchat.com). Most injections were demonstrated on only one or two interfaces (overwhelmingly ChatGPT and GPT-3/GPT-4), so generalization to other LLM interfaces is assumed rather than shown. Indirect injections were not tested at all. There is an internal inconsistency in counts: the Findings text and conclusion say seventeen verified variations, while Appendix A's text says 'fifteen prompt injections that had multiple sources or were successfully verified' even though its Table 4 lists 17 numbered entries; the conclusion also states 'four and six separate classes... respectively' which mislabels direct (6) vs indirect (4) relative to the rest of the paper.

datasets_used: No ML dataset. Corpus of 123 papers found via Google Scholar keyword search 'prompt injection' (May 2022 - Sept 2023)
Grey-literature sources: jailbreakchat.com, Reddit communities ChatGPTJailbreak and ChatGPT, plus Google/arXiv/GitHub/Medium/Twitter searches
17 compiled example injections listed in Appendix A / Table 4, each with target and primary source
quantitative_results: 123 papers identified that discuss prompt injection as an adversarial/security threat
17 verified prompt-injection variations (Findings/Conclusion); Appendix A text states 15 sufficiently documented/verified while Table 4 numbers 17 entries
10 total classes: 6 direct classes and 4 indirect classes
Direct classes: Double Character, Virtualization, Obfuscation, Payload Splitting, Adversarial Suffix, Instruction Manipulation
Indirect classes: Active Injections, Passive Injections, User-driven Injections, Virtual Prompt Injection
Example targets in Table 4 include ChatGPT, GPT-3, GPT-4, Bing AI, Bard, Claude, LLM-assisted apps, external websites, and LLM training data
No attack-success-rate, accuracy, or defense-effectiveness numbers are reported (non-experimental study)
baselines_compared: None. This is a taxonomy/synthesis paper with no experimental comparison or baseline systems. It builds on the direct/indirect dichotomy from Greshake et al. (2023) and organizes attacks named in Perez & Ribeiro (2022), Kang et al. (2023), Zou et al. (2023), Shen et al. (2023), and Yan et al. (2023).
